text: Hello friends, I am sharing a short technical video of laparoscopic repair of peptic perforation. A forty one year old male patient, chronic smoker and occasional alcoholic presented to emergency with severe pain abdomen for one day mainly in the upper part of the abdomen. On examination there was guarding rigidity and tenderness over right hypochondrium and epigastric region and there was associated obliterated liver dullness. So on straight x-ray abdomen there was free air under both domes of diaphragm and so my provisional diagnosis was it is a case of hollow viscous perforation. So, I plan to go ahead with the diagnostic laparoscopy and proceed accordingly.

So, my OT setup, there should be always in such kind of cases, there should be always two monitors. One should be at the head end and one should be at the leg end and mainly because of ease of giving peritoneal lavage because this is case of parvoid peritoneitis. Surgeon should stand on between the legs, camera assistant on the right side of the patient and right side of the surgeon and Scrapna should be on the left side of the surgeon and Anastasis on the head end. So this is my standard roti setup for this kind of cases. And if you go ahead with the port position, I did viris, neocortinum with viris needle through the umbilicus and then converted it to a ten millimeter port through which it will be my, through which I kept my camera as a camera port. and the left hand working port is in the right hypochondrial region, this forefinger below the right subcostal margin. There should be two right hand working port because you have to change between the shuffle between the two ports while suturing the defect. So, five millimeter port over the left subcostal region and five millimeter port at the left level of umbilicus on the left parallax position and there was an associated One more port in the right anterior axillary line just below the costal margin which will act as a liver retraction port. So this is my standard port position for this kind of upper abdominal or stomach upper GI surgery is a standard five port insertion.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes tissue flakes and fluid in the right subhepatic and subdiaphragmatic areas. After suctioning the fluid, the exploration reveals a small perforation on the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, with visible red mucosa and gastric contents leaking. The surrounding tissue flakes are cleared, and the margins of the perforation are trimmed, with some tissue sent for biopsy.

The surgeon clears the flakes around the perforation site on the duodenum and begins suturing using 2-0 PDS. Three stay sutures are placed: one at the proximal margin, one through the middle, and one at the lower margin of the perforation. Each suture is taken through and through with deep bites to ensure secure closure of the defect.

The surgeon uses an instrument from the right anterior axillary port for liver retraction, providing exposure to take full-thickness bites through the defect. Three sutures are placed over the defect area. The surgeon selects a well-vascularized and mobile omentum to cover the defect, asking the assistant to hold the omentum in place while securing the first suture knot. After securing the first knot, the omentum remains in position, allowing the surgeon to proceed with additional sutures without instrument crowding.

The surgeon secures the knot and trims the excess suture before taking the second bite. The distal suture is tightened to prevent loosening, while the second suture holds the omentum in place. Care is taken to ensure all knots sit properly on the omentum without compromising its blood supply. After suturing, thorough peritoneal lavage is performed under laparoscopic vision.

so there will not be any compromise there is a notion between the surgeons among the surgeons that in laparoscopy you cannot give proper lavage but that is not true rather the opposite i feel because with laparoscopy in a magnified view and in seeing a hd vision in this monitor and with nice suction apparatus available you can give nice lavage all around all along the peritoneal cavity but you should have one thing inside your operation theory that you should there should be two monitors should be available one on the head end during the lavage given of the upper part of the peritoneal cavity upper abdominal cavity and one monitor should be used for the while giving lavage for the lower abdominal cavity And the surgeon should also change the position. Surgeon and camera assistant should also change the position during giving the lavage accordingly. So while giving the peritoneal lavage in the pelvis, the surgeon should stand on the head end side and camera assistant should be on the left side of the surgeon. And while giving on the right and left paraculic gutter, the surgeon should, surgeon and camera assistant should stand on the sides of the patient. So peritoneal lavage should be given with nice, warm, normal saline. It should not be too cold or it should not be too hot. And again, the principle is to give lavage until and unless the returning fluid is clear. So that is the indication of adequate lavage which is given. So once you are happy with the peritoneal lavage, then you have to think of placing drain.

The surgeon observes unduly dilated small bowel loops, a common occurrence due to paralytic ileus from perforated peritonitis. Careful bowel retraction is performed using atraumatic instruments to avoid damage while preparing for lavage.

The surgeon places two drains after completing the lavage: one drain in the sub-hepatic area through the right anterior axillary port, and another 24 French drain into the pelvis through the left pararectal five millimeter port.
